Apophis.description: The most famous asteroid in the solar system, and for good reason. In 2004, newly discovered Apophis briefly carried a 1-in-37 chance of hitting Earth in 2029 — the highest impact probability ever calculated for a known asteroid. That threat was ruled out, but the April 13, 2029 flyby remains extraordinary: at 31,600 km from Earth's surface, Apophis will pass below geostationary satellites and be visible to the naked eye as a moving point of light crossing the sky. Over 50,000 satellites orbit above where Apophis will travel. Named after the ancient Egyptian god of chaos.
Apophis.size_note: 340 meters wide — about the size of the Empire State Building laid on its side
Apophis.next_approach: April 13, 2029 — 31,600 km from Earth (below geostationary orbit)

Bennu.description: The most studied potentially hazardous asteroid in existence, and the most personally familiar — a 70-gram sample of Bennu arrived on Earth in 2023 via NASA's OSIRIS-REx capsule, the first US asteroid sample return ever. The samples contained carbon-rich organics and water-bearing clay minerals, the kind of chemistry that may have seeded life on early Earth. Bennu has a 0.057% cumulative impact probability through the year 2300, making it the most statistically dangerous known asteroid on a multi-century timescale. Named after the Egyptian mythological bird associated with the sun, creation, and rebirth.
Bennu.size_note: 484 meters wide — taller than the Empire State Building
Bennu.mission: OSIRIS-REx (NASA) — sample collected 2020, returned to Earth 2023
Bennu.next_approach: 2135: closest approach in its current orbital cycle

Ryugu.description: A spinning top-shaped asteroid that changed what we know about the early solar system. Japan's Hayabusa2 probe orbited Ryugu for 18 months, then shot a copper cannonball into its surface to excavate a fresh crater and collect subsurface material. The samples returned to Earth in 2020 contained 20 amino acids — the building blocks of proteins — along with liquid water trapped in minerals. Ryugu's surface is covered in boulders and almost no fine regolith, something no model predicted. Named after the undersea palace in a Japanese folktale whose visitor returns to find centuries have passed.
Ryugu.mission: Hayabusa2 (JAXA) — orbited 2018–2019, samples returned to Earth December 2020

Itokawa.description: The asteroid that made history as the first ever to yield a sample that was returned to Earth. Japan's original Hayabusa probe landed twice on Itokawa in 2005, collected microscopic dust grains, and returned them to Australia in 2010 despite a series of near-catastrophic failures including a fuel leak, loss of reaction wheels, and a thruster malfunction. The peanut-shaped asteroid appears to be a 'rubble pile' — two distinct lobes loosely held together by their own gravity, not solid rock as expected. Each grain of Itokawa dust tells a story spanning 4.5 billion years of solar system history.

Didymos.description: The site of the first planetary defense test in history. On September 26, 2022, NASA's DART spacecraft — a spacecraft the size of a vending machine — slammed into Dimorphos, the small moon orbiting Didymos, at 6.1 km/s. The impact shortened Dimorphos's orbital period by 33 minutes — 25 times more effective than models predicted, largely because the impact excavated a massive plume of ejecta that provided additional thrust. ESA's Hera mission is now en route to survey the aftermath. The experiment proved that kinetic impactors can deflect an asteroid — the first verified planetary defense technique.
Didymos.size_note: 780 meters wide, with moon Dimorphos at 160 meters
Didymos.mission: DART (NASA) — kinetic impactor September 2022; Hera (ESA) — survey mission arriving 2026
Didymos.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Phaethon.description: Every December, Earth passes through a river of debris shed by Phaethon, producing the Geminid meteor shower — the year's most prolific, with up to 150 shooting stars per hour. Phaethon is the only known asteroid confirmed to produce a meteor shower. Its orbit takes it closer to the Sun than any other named asteroid, reaching temperatures of 800°C — hot enough to crack rock and possibly vaporize sodium, which may be what creates the debris trail. Japan's DESTINY+ mission is en route for a 2028 flyby to photograph its sun-scorched surface up close. Named after the son of the sun god Helios, who briefly drove the solar chariot and burned the Earth.
Phaethon.next_approach: No Earth close approach before 2040, but produces the annual Geminid meteor shower every December

Florence.description: The largest asteroid to fly past Earth in modern recorded history. Florence's September 2017 flyby at 7 million km (18 lunar distances) was the closest pass by such a large object since radar astronomy began. During the flyby, ground-based radar imaging revealed a surprise: Florence has not one but two tiny moons orbiting it, making it only the third known Amor-class asteroid with moons. At nearly 5 km wide, a Florence-scale impact would be a global catastrophe — and the 2017 flyby brought the closest such object has been to Earth in over 400 years. Named after Florence Nightingale.
Florence.size_note: 4.9 km wide — largest near-Earth flyby in recorded radar history
Florence.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Toutatis.description: One of the most unusual asteroids in the solar system — Toutatis tumbles in a slow, chaotic rotation with no defined north pole and no fixed spin axis. It wobbles through space like a poorly thrown football. This chaotic tumble is the result of a complex orbital resonance and possibly a past collision. China's Chang'e 2 spacecraft flew within 3.2 km of Toutatis in December 2012, producing the first close-up images — a lumpy, elongated object covered in craters and boulders. At 5.4 km, an impact would be regionally catastrophic. Toutatis makes unusually frequent close approaches to Earth due to its resonant orbit. Named after the Celtic deity worshipped by Asterix and Obelix.
Toutatis.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Geographos.description: The most elongated major asteroid known — Geographos has a length-to-width ratio of nearly 2.8:1, shaped like a stretched potato or a rugby ball with the ends pinched. It rotates every 5.2 hours, and its extreme elongation may be a result of a slow tidal distortion during a past close Earth encounter. In 1994, NASA's Clementine spacecraft was scheduled to fly past Geographos after its Moon mission — it would have been the first dedicated asteroid flyby — but a thruster malfunction prevented the encounter. Ground-based radar finally mapped its dramatic shape in 1994. Named after the National Geographic Society, whose donations funded the Palomar Sky Survey that discovered it.
Geographos.size_note: 2.56 km long — one of the most elongated known asteroids
Geographos.mission: Clementine flyby planned 1994 (aborted due to thruster failure)

Icarus.description: The first asteroid ever discovered whose orbit brings it closer to the Sun than Mercury. At its closest solar approach, Icarus reaches temperatures over 500°C — its surface glowing red-hot. Discovered in 1949, it became famous in 1967 when MIT students were given a real engineering challenge: design humanity's actual response if Icarus were on a collision course with Earth. The resulting 'Project Icarus' study proposed six Saturn V rockets carrying nuclear warheads — the first serious scientific analysis of asteroid deflection ever conducted. During its celebrated June 1968 flyby at 6.4 million km, it was covered live by the press as a pop-science sensation. Named after the mythological figure who flew too close to the Sun.
Icarus.size_note: 1 km wide — surface temperature exceeds 500°C at perihelion
Icarus.mission: Subject of MIT's 1967 Project Icarus deflection study
Icarus.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Hermes.description: The original 'lost' asteroid — the one that started the world worrying about asteroid impacts. When Hermes was discovered in 1937, it approached Earth at just twice the lunar distance — one of the closest known approaches in the 20th century — then vanished before astronomers could fully compute its orbit. It remained lost for 66 years, haunting the popular imagination as 'the asteroid we can't find.' When rediscovered in 2003, astronomers realized it is actually a binary asteroid with a companion of similar size. Named after the messenger god who guided souls to the underworld — and who was known for disappearing and reappearing without warning.
Hermes.size_note: 330 meters wide — binary system with a roughly equal companion
Hermes.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Castalia.description: The first asteroid whose shape was directly imaged by radar — and the discovery that changed how astronomers think about asteroids. When Arecibo Observatory pointed its radar at Castalia during its 1989 flyby, the return signal revealed two distinct lobes connected at a narrow neck: a contact binary, two objects that merged so slowly they stuck together without shattering. This was the first concrete proof that asteroids could be 'rubble piles' or merged objects rather than solid monoliths. Castalia's discovery directly influenced models of Ryugu, Bennu, and many other asteroids subsequently found to have the same bilobate structure.
Castalia.size_note: 1.4 km wide — contact binary, two lobes merged at the neck
Castalia.mission: Arecibo radar imaging 1989 — first asteroid shape reconstruction from radar

Golevka.description: The asteroid that proved the Sun pushes asteroids off course. Scientists predicted Golevka's 2003 position based on pure gravitational models, then compared it to where it actually was — and found it 15 km off. The discrepancy was the first direct measurement of the Yarkovsky effect: sunlight absorbed by an asteroid on one side and re-radiated as heat on the other creates a tiny but cumulative thrust. Over decades, this shifts an asteroid's orbit measurably. Golevka's name is a portmanteau of the three radar observatories that tracked it: Goldstone (USA), Evpatoria (Ukraine), and Kashima (Japan). A genuinely multinational discovery.
Golevka.mission: Arecibo radar imaging 1995; Yarkovsky effect measured 1999–2003
Golevka.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Apollo.description: The asteroid that named an entire class. When Apollo was discovered in 1932, it crossed Earth's orbit — the first asteroid confirmed to do so — prompting astronomers to create the 'Apollo' category for Earth-crossing asteroids. Apollo was itself lost shortly after discovery, then rediscovered in 1973. The class now contains over 15,000 members, making it the largest group of near-Earth asteroids — all named after this single 1.5 km wide rock that surprised astronomers by casually crossing Earth's path during the Depression era.
Apollo.size_note: 1.5 km wide — the name-giving prototype of Earth-crossing asteroids
Apollo.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Moshup.description: A binary asteroid system where the main body and its moon Squannit are so similar in size that the system looks more like a double asteroid than a primary with a satellite. Moshup and Squannit orbit each other every 17.4 hours while both rotate rapidly. The binary was discovered during the 1999 close flyby when radar imaging revealed the unexpected companion. This kind of close binary system likely formed when rapid rotation caused the asteroid to shed material that coalesced into a moon — a process that may be common but is rarely caught in such perfect mid-formation balance. Named after a giant from Wampanoag Native American mythology.
Moshup.size_note: 1.3 km wide, with moon Squannit at 0.4 km
Moshup.mission: Arecibo radar imaging 1999 — revealed binary nature

Nereus.description: From an engineering perspective, Nereus is one of the most reachable destinations in the solar system. Its delta-v requirement — the fuel cost to fly a spacecraft there — is comparable to reaching the Moon in some launch windows. This accessibility made it a top candidate for asteroid mining studies and sample return missions. Japan originally planned to visit it with Hayabusa before selecting Itokawa instead. NASA considered it for multiple mission proposals. Despite constant attention, no spacecraft has yet reached Nereus. It remains a tantalizing destination for the first commercial asteroid extraction venture, should one ever launch. Named after the Greek 'Old Man of the Sea.'
Nereus.size_note: 330 meters wide — one of the most accessible asteroids from Earth
Nereus.mission: Multiple planned missions proposed, none yet flown

Wilson-Harrington.description: The only object in the solar system listed in both the asteroid catalog and the comet catalog simultaneously. When first observed in 1949, Wilson-Harrington showed a distinct coma and dust tail — classic comet behavior. It was catalogued as comet P/1949 W1 and then lost. When rediscovered in 1979 as an unremarkable asteroid, astronomers realized it was the same object — now exhibiting no cometary activity whatsoever. What happened? The leading theory: Wilson-Harrington exhausted its volatile surface ices and is now a dead cometary nucleus masquerading as an asteroid. Thousands of 'asteroids' may share this hidden identity. Named after its two discoverers.
Wilson-Harrington.size_note: 4 km wide — a dead comet nucleus disguised as an asteroid

Adonis.description: One of the founding members of the near-Earth asteroid family, Adonis was discovered in 1936 during a close approach to Earth, then lost for 41 years before being rediscovered in 1977. At its 1936 close approach, it passed only 1.5 million km from Earth — about 4 lunar distances. Its recovery was a landmark in asteroid tracking history. Named after the mortal beloved by Aphrodite in Greek mythology, famous for his beauty — and for dying and returning to life in a seasonal cycle not unlike Adonis the asteroid: appearing, disappearing, and reappearing across decades.

Asclepius.description: The asteroid that delivered the world's first genuine asteroid scare. When 1989 FC (later named Asclepius) crossed Earth's orbit in March 1989, astronomers calculated it had passed Earth just 6 hours earlier at a distance of 690,000 km — less than twice the lunar distance. Had it arrived 6 hours earlier in its journey, it would have hit Earth. At 300 meters wide, the impact would have released energy equivalent to a 2,000 megaton explosion, comparable to thousands of Hiroshima bombs. The 1989 near-miss was largely unknown to the public at the time and was only fully analyzed weeks later. Named after the Greek god of medicine and healing.
Asclepius.size_note: 300 meters wide — would have released 2,000 megatons of energy on impact
Asclepius.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Oljato.description: Oljato is an asteroid that might actually be a dead comet — and the magnetic field disturbances it leaves behind may prove it. When spacecraft fly through Oljato's orbital path, they detect anomalous magnetic field fluctuations. The leading explanation: Oljato was once an active comet that shed charged particles now drifting along its former trajectory, like a ghost comet leaving an electromagnetic trail. The Ulysses spacecraft detected this signature twice in 1992 and 1996. If correct, Oljato gives scientists a way to detect former comets that have shed all visual evidence of their icy past. Named after a Navajo sacred site.
Oljato.size_note: 1.8 km wide — possibly a formerly active comet
Oljato.mission: Ulysses spacecraft detected its magnetic signature 1992 and 1996
Oljato.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Midas.description: A 3.4 km Apollo-class asteroid named after King Midas of Greek mythology — the ruler cursed to turn everything he touched to gold. Midas the asteroid touches something too: Earth's orbital path, crossing it regularly in an orbit that will bring it close to our planet on a repeating cycle. At 3.4 km, a Midas-scale impact would be a continent-scale catastrophe, releasing energy comparable to all nuclear weapons ever built multiplied many times over. Its size puts it in the range that formed the Chicxulub crater that ended the dinosaurs.
Midas.size_note: 3.4 km wide — above the extinction-level threshold

Cuno.description: One of the larger potentially hazardous asteroids and among the fastest-rotating well-characterized ones. Cuno's rapid spin and high reflectivity (albedo) suggest a rocky, consolidated object rather than a loose rubble pile — which would make deflection via kinetic impactor more challenging, as a solid object transmits impact momentum differently than a fragmented pile. At 3.65 km, it exceeds the rough threshold for global climate impact. Named after Cuno Hoffmeister, the German astronomer who made major contributions to variable star and meteor research.
Cuno.size_note: 3.65 km wide — above global climate impact threshold
Cuno.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Cacus.description: An Apollo-class asteroid notable for being one of the faster-rotating objects in the near-Earth population, completing a full rotation in just 3.75 hours. Rapid rotators are scientifically interesting because above a certain spin rate, a loosely bound rubble pile would fly apart — so very fast spinners are likely monolithic solid rocks, not aggregates. Knowing the internal structure affects how we'd deflect it. Named after the fire-breathing, cattle-rustling giant of Roman mythology, son of Vulcan, who was slain by Hercules after stealing some of the Sun god's cattle.

Tantalus.description: Named after the mythological Greek king condemned for eternity to stand in a pool of water beneath fruit trees, unable to reach either the water or the fruit — forever tantalized. Tantalus the asteroid is similarly elusive: a 1.65 km Apollo-class object that crosses Earth's orbit regularly but keeps its closest approaches just far enough away to remain frustratingly beyond easy spacecraft reach. It crosses Earth's orbit on a 1.5-year period and has been studied extensively by ground-based radar but never visited.

Dionysus.description: A binary asteroid system — Dionysus hosts a small moon orbiting the main body. Binary asteroids are scientifically invaluable because the orbital dynamics of the moon allow precise measurement of the primary's mass — information that's almost impossible to obtain for a single asteroid without a spacecraft flyby. Dionysus's binary nature was suspected from lightcurve analysis showing periodic brightness variations. Named after the Greek god of wine, ecstasy, and ritual madness — a deity associated with duality and transformation, fitting for an asteroid that turned out to be two objects rather than one.
Dionysus.size_note: 1.5 km wide, with a small moon
Dionysus.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Zephyr.description: A 2 km Apollo-class asteroid named after Zephyros, the ancient Greek personification of the west wind — gentle, warm, and seasonal. Zephyr the asteroid is considerably less gentle: at 2 km wide, an impact would release energy roughly 100,000 times the Hiroshima bomb, enough to collapse civilization in the affected hemisphere. Like the wind it's named for, it crosses Earth's path predictably, seasonally, on an orbital period that brings it back to the same region of space every few years.

Mithra.description: Mithra is one of the slowest rotating named PHAs — it takes approximately 67.5 hours to complete a full rotation, more than two and a half Earth days. Unusually slow rotation is often a sign that an object has been 'despun' by tidal forces during a past close planetary flyby, or that the Yarkovsky-O'Keefe-Radzievskii-Paddack (YORP) effect — radiation pressure from sunlight — has gradually slowed its spin over millions of years. At 1.85 km, it's large enough to cause regional catastrophe. Named after the Indo-Iranian deity of covenants, light, and truth — a god who was thought to see all things.
Mithra.size_note: 1.85 km wide — unusually slow rotator at 67 hours per revolution

Zoozve.description: The asteroid with the internet's favorite origin story. In 2022, a space artist painted a poster of the solar system that labeled an object near Venus as 'Zoozve' — a garbled version of its designation '2002 VE68' that a child had apparently misread. When podcaster Latif Nasser investigated the mysterious name, he eventually reached the scientist who officially named it Zoozve in 2024 in honor of the story. But Zoozve itself is scientifically fascinating: it's a quasi-satellite of Venus — an asteroid that appears to orbit Venus but actually orbits the Sun in a resonance that keeps it near Venus for centuries at a time. The first object ever confirmed in this orbital relationship with Venus.

Hypnos.description: Hypnos is suspected to be the dead nucleus of an extinct comet — a body that exhausted its ices over millions of perihelion passes and now drifts in slumber, showing no outgassing or cometary activity. Its low albedo (very dark surface, reflecting only 4% of sunlight) matches the composition of cometary nuclei, and its orbit is consistent with a formerly active comet that evolved into a near-Earth object over geological time. If correct, Hypnos represents the fate of many comets — and suggests the boundary between 'comet' and 'asteroid' is far blurrier than astronomers once believed. Named after the Greek god of sleep.
Hypnos.size_note: 900 meters wide — possibly an extinct comet nucleus
Hypnos.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Lugh.description: A recently named Apollo-class asteroid honoring Lugh, the Celtic god of the sun, light, and craftsmanship — a master of all arts who won his place among the gods by declaring himself uniquely skilled in every discipline simultaneously. Lugh the asteroid is noteworthy for its relatively frequent Earth encounters due to its orbital resonance with our planet. At 1.4 km, it represents the boundary between objects that would cause regional catastrophe and those capable of global climate effects.

Vishnu.description: A fast-rotating Aten-class asteroid named after the Hindu preserver of the universe — one of the three principal deities of Hinduism. Vishnu the asteroid completes a rotation in just 2.0 hours, near the critical limit where a loosely bound rubble pile would begin to shed material from centrifugal force. Its rapid spin suggests it may be a monolithic fragment rather than an aggregate, which has implications for how it would respond to a deflection attempt. Small but fast — an asteroid that takes its divine name's association with cosmic cycles seriously.
Vishnu.size_note: 420 meters wide — extremely fast rotator at 2 hours per revolution

Orpheus.description: A small Apollo-class asteroid named after the legendary Thracian musician whose music was so beautiful it charmed stones, rivers, and even the gods of the underworld. Orpheus the asteroid is considerably less harmonious — at 300 meters wide and an orbit that regularly intersects Earth's, it falls in the size range capable of destroying a major city or triggering a tsunami if it struck an ocean. Its November 2025 approach at 15 lunar distances is one of the closer encounters of any named PHA in the near term.

Hathor.description: One of the smaller members of the rare Aten class — asteroids whose orbits lie mostly inside Earth's orbit. Aten asteroids are difficult to observe because they're usually visible only briefly near the horizon. Hathor was the fifth Aten-class asteroid discovered and remains one of the few achieving genuinely close Earth approaches. Named after the Egyptian goddess of love, beauty, music, and the sky — one of the most widely worshipped deities of ancient Egypt, associated with joy and the cycle of the sun.
Hathor.size_note: 300 meters wide — Aten class, spends most time inside Earth's orbit

Khufu.description: An Aten-class asteroid named after Khufu — the Egyptian pharaoh who ordered the Great Pyramid of Giza built, one of the largest structures ever created by human hands. Khufu the asteroid spends most of its orbit inside Earth's path, making it harder to detect and track than typical near-Earth objects. At 700 meters — larger than the pyramid's base — Khufu crosses from 'city-destroyer' scale to potentially civilization-disrupting scale. Aten asteroids like Khufu represent a particularly challenging population because their sun-ward orbits make them harder to intercept with warning.
Khufu.size_note: 700 meters wide — Aten class, orbits mostly inside Earth's path
Khufu.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Agni.description: An Aten-class asteroid named after Agni, the ancient Hindu god of fire — one of the most important deities in the Vedic tradition, serving as messenger between humans and the divine through the sacred flame. Agni makes several noteworthy approaches to Earth on a recurring cycle. At 462 meters, it sits in the size range that planetary defense scientists consider the primary target for early warning systems — large enough to be catastrophic, small enough that an early deflection mission with sufficient lead time could succeed.

Anagolay.description: Named after the Filipino goddess of lost things — a deity who helps recover what has gone missing. There's something fitting about naming an Apollo-class asteroid after her: near-Earth asteroids are frequently 'lost' between apparitions when they travel too far or move into unfavorable observing geometries, only to be recovered years or decades later. Anagolay is a 500-meter Apollo-class object that crosses Earth's orbit and makes periodic appearances in the inner solar system.
Anagolay.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Tomaiyowit.description: Named after a figure in Luiseño Native American mythology — an important deity in the cosmology of the Luiseño people of California. Tomaiyowit is a 738-meter Apollo-class asteroid that makes regular inner solar system crossings. At its size, it falls in the range where a kinetic impactor deflection mission would be feasible with sufficient warning — the range that planetary defense organizations currently prioritize for tracking and characterization.

Svarog.description: Named after Svarog, the Slavic god of celestial fire and the heavenly forge — the divine smith who created the world and gifted fire to humanity. Svarog makes a notably close approach to Earth in March 2027 at 19 lunar distances, one of the closer encounters of any named PHA in the near term. At this distance, ground-based radar could produce detailed shape models. At 665 meters, it's large enough that a kinetic impactor deflection mission would require significant lead time and mission scale.
Svarog.next_approach: March 2027 at 19 lunar distances — close enough for detailed radar imaging

Cardea.description: The smallest named PHA in the tracking database — just 163 meters wide, roughly the length of a city block. Named after Cardea, the Roman goddess of door hinges and handles, the guardian of thresholds. Cardea the asteroid crosses Earth's orbital threshold regularly, and at its size represents the lower boundary of objects that planetary defense systems prioritize. A 163-meter impactor would release roughly the energy of the 1908 Tunguska event — which flattened 2,000 square kilometers of Siberian forest. Small by PHA standards. Not small by human standards.
Cardea.size_note: 163 meters wide — about the length of a city block

Aristaeus.description: Named after Aristaeus, the ancient Greek deity of beekeeping, cheese-making, herding, and the art of olive-growing — a patron of rural crafts and the protector of flocks. Aristaeus the asteroid is an Apollo-class object that crosses Earth's orbit, tracked as a potentially hazardous asteroid due to its orbital characteristics. While no close approaches are projected before 2040, its Earth-crossing orbit ensures it will feature in future close approach tables as orbital solutions improve.
Aristaeus.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Pan.description: Named after Pan, the Greek god of the wild, nature, shepherds, and rustic music — the half-goat deity whose sudden appearances inspired 'panic' in those he startled. Pan the asteroid crosses Earth's orbit and makes recurring inner solar system approaches. It occupies the size range where planetary defense is most active: large enough to cause regional catastrophe, small enough that a deflection mission with sufficient warning would be technically feasible.

Ptah.description: Named after Ptah, the ancient Egyptian creator god who fashioned the universe through thought and spoken word — the patron of craftsmen, architects, and all makers of things. Ptah the asteroid is an Apollo-class object occupying the size range where a regional impact would be catastrophic. Ptah the creator deity was believed to speak existence into being; Ptah the asteroid, if it ever arrived, would speak destruction through shockwave, fire, and debris.

Minos.description: Named after Minos, the legendary king of Crete who commissioned the labyrinth and whose name gives us the word 'Minoan.' Minos the king was a judge of the dead in the underworld. Minos the asteroid is an Apollo-class PHA with a relatively close approach in February 2025 at 39 lunar distances, making it one of the near-term encounters worth tracking. At 400 meters, it falls squarely in the 'city to region destroying' size range that planetary defense organizations monitor most actively.

Illapa.description: Named after Illapa, the Incan god of thunder, lightning, and weather — a major deity in the Inca Empire who controlled rain and was depicted as a man in shining garments hurling lightning bolts. Illapa was often invoked before battle. Illapa the asteroid is an Apollo-class PHA that crosses Earth's orbit and makes periodic inner solar system approaches, representing the kinds of objects that ancient cultures might well have interpreted as divine messages had they appeared in the sky.

Mjolnir.description: Named after Mjolnir, Thor's hammer in Norse mythology — the weapon that could level mountains and was so heavy only Thor could lift it. Mjolnir the asteroid is small by PHA standards — about 150 meters — but even a 150-meter impact would release more energy than all nuclear weapons ever detonated combined, roughly equivalent to the Tunguska event. An object the size of a city block, named after the most destructive weapon in mythology. The Norse gods chose their names well.
Mjolnir.next_approach: March 2032 at 17 lunar distances — close radar characterization opportunity

Tatsunokingo.description: A Japanese-named Apollo-class potentially hazardous asteroid. 'Tatsunokingo' draws from Japanese naming tradition for celestial objects, part of the global effort to bring diverse cultural perspectives to asteroid nomenclature. The International Astronomical Union has increasingly named newly characterized objects after figures from non-Western mythologies, acknowledging that the sky belongs to all human cultures. Tatsunokingo crosses Earth's orbit and is tracked as a potentially hazardous object due to its size and orbital parameters.
Tatsunokingo.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Dryope.description: Named after Dryope, a figure from Greek mythology who was transformed into a tree by the Hamadryads (tree nymphs) after she picked a lotus flower sacred to them. The story of being transformed from a human into something rooted and immovable carries a certain poignancy when applied to an asteroid — an object that has been on the same orbital path for millions of years, locked in its trajectory the way Dryope was locked into her tree. A 300-meter Apollo-class PHA crossing Earth's path.
Dryope.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Akhenaten.description: Named after the Egyptian pharaoh Akhenaten — the revolutionary ruler who abolished Egypt's polytheistic religion, declared the sun disc Aten the sole god, and moved the capital city. He was so controversial that after his death, his successors attempted to erase his memory entirely, destroying his monuments and removing his name from records. Akhenaten the asteroid is an Aten-class object — its name deliberately echoing the class designation — spending most of its orbit inside Earth's path around the Sun, making it one of the harder PHAs to observe.

Jormungandr.description: Named after Jörmungandr, the World Serpent of Norse mythology — a serpent so vast it encircles the entire Earth and bites its own tail. According to Norse myth, when Jörmungandr releases its tail, Ragnarök begins. Jörmungandr the asteroid is an Apollo-class object whose orbit encircles and crosses Earth's path. At 300 meters, it sits in the range where a direct impact would cause a catastrophe equivalent to thousands of nuclear weapons. The mythological resonance of a world-encircling serpent applied to an Earth-crossing asteroid is not accidental.

Nabu.description: Named after Nabu, the Babylonian god of wisdom, writing, and scribes — the deity who recorded the fates of all beings on clay tablets in his heavenly library. In Babylonian cosmology, Nabu knew the destiny of every star, every person, every event. Nabu the asteroid has an orbital destiny scientists are actively computing — tracking its path through space to determine whether Earth's fate and this asteroid's path ever intersect.
Nabu.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040

Flordeneu.description: Named after Flordeneu, a character in Catalan mythology — a fairy queen who lived in the Pyrenean mountains. The Catalan naming reflects the International Astronomical Union's effort to draw asteroid names from diverse cultural traditions worldwide. Flordeneu makes a close approach to Earth in October 2038 at 12 lunar distances — one of the closer encounters in this timeframe — making it a potential target for radar characterization that could refine its orbit and size estimate significantly.
Flordeneu.next_approach: October 2038 at 12 lunar distances — relatively close encounter

Curupira.description: Named after Curupira, a protective spirit of the Brazilian rainforest in Tupi-Guaraní indigenous mythology — depicted as a child with flaming red hair and feet turned backward, who confuses hunters trying to harm the forest by leaving false tracks. Curupira exists to protect the forest. Curupira the asteroid crosses Earth's orbit, a reminder that the solar system contains thousands of objects whose stories remain untold until astronomers give them names from the full breadth of human storytelling.
Curupira.next_approach: No close approach to Earth before 2040
